pages.text: with the composition shown in Table 2. Pozolanic Portland cement, medium quartz sand, basaltic gravel, recycled aggregates, both ﬁne and coarse and water were used. Tables 3–5 show, respectively, the grain size composition, aggregate characterizations and the ce- ment used. 2.3. Production of the concretes Initially an experimental dosage was prepared and the slump was set at 100 ± 20 mm. A composition of the reference concrete was established from this dosage and then the compositions were established for concretes with recycled aggregates, considering the replacement percentage and volume compensation, calculated through Eq. (3). Table 6 presents the quantities of materials used to produce the concretes. Mar ¼ Manx car can ð3Þ where Mar is the mass of recycled aggregate (g); Man the mass of natural aggregate (g); car the speciﬁc mass of recycled aggregate (g/cm3) and cna is the speciﬁc mass of natural aggregate (g/cm 3). The water absorption of the aggregates was taken into account by pre-humidifying them 15 min before the mixing process. Sev- eral authors [10,11] stand out the necessity of pre-humidiﬁcation of the aggregates however; there is no recommendation of the duration of this procedure. This procedure aims at partially com- pensating material absorption, keeping the mixing water from being absorbed by the aggregate. The ﬁne aggregate received 55% of the total absorption while, for the coarse aggregate, 70% of the total absorption rate was used. After the pre-humidiﬁcation procedure the materials were placed in the cement mixer and mixed. No plastifying additive was used in order to also verify the inﬂuence of recycled aggre- gates on the water/cement ratio of the concrete. After the initial water/cement ratios (initial w/c) water was added to the mixture until it reached the deﬁned 100 ± 20 mm slump, resulting in a new w/c (real w/c) for each mixture. Once the established slump was reached thirteen cylindrical specimens (95 /C2 190 mm, diame- ter /C2 height) were molded for each mixture, according to ASTM C 192 (2007). These were cured under laboratory conditions in the ﬁrst 24 h and after demolding they were placed in a humidity chamber with relative humidity above 95% and at a temperature of 23 ± 1 /C176C. 2.4. Test methods The results of the response variables collected in this study are presented in Tables 7 and 8 . The values observed are the data obtained in the experiment and the forecast values are the results obtained by the model. The adopted test procedures are as follows. 2.4.1. Mechanical properties The mechanical properties were evaluated when the specimens were 28 days old. The compressive strength test was carried out with four specimens per mixture, according to the standards estab- lished in ASTM C 39. Two of these specimens were used to forecast the break load, as recommended by the test procedure to deter- mine the deformation module. Following the ASTM C 496 recom- mendations, two specimens per mixture were submitted to tensile strength tests. The test to determine the elastic modulus of the concretes fol- lowed the ASTM C 469 procedure for ﬁve specimens per mixture. Two of them were broken by compression, as set out in the stan- dard, for the break load forecast. The test was carried out by apply- ing increasing loading at a velocity of 0.50 ± 0.05 MPa/s, with pauses every 60 s to measure deformation. The deformations were measured with an electric extensometer. 2.4.2. Water absorption tests of concretes

3.2. Exploration of the response surfaces The analyses to study the durability properties were carried out aiming at obtaining the lower is better response. On the other hand, the study of the mechanical properties, sought the higher is better response. The models that originated the surfaces were presented in Section 3.1. According to the analyses of the graphs in the ﬁgure and the response surfaces, it can be seen that the mechanical properties de- crease when the recycled aggregate content of the concrete in- creases and that therefore durability also decreases. The type of recycled aggregate does not inﬂuence the elastic modulus, but coarse aggregate has a greater negative inﬂuence on the compres- sive strength and tensile strength than ﬁne aggregate, while in the durability properties ﬁne aggregate has a greater negative inﬂu- ence than coarse aggregate. 3.2.1. Mechanical properties Fig. 8 shows the response surfaces concerning the compressive strength of the concretes. The surface shows (a) that the increase of the recycled aggregate content (ﬁne and coarse) tends to reduce the concrete resistance, which is not desirable. The highest resis- tances are shown in concretes without recycled aggregates (CRA level /C0 1 e FRA level /C0 1). When 100% of FRA and CRA are added to the mixture the con- crete resistance reduction is 18% and 24% respectively. Loss of resistance, when working with 100% substitution of natural aggre- gates by recycled ones, was also found in the literature [7,10,19].A resistance reduction of about 20% was found for low w/c ratios (0.45), in concretes with 100% FRA and 100% CRA [19]. According to Rahal [19], concretes with 100% coarse recycled aggregate showed a reduction in compressive strength between 9% and 45%. In this sense [7] found that the largest negative inﬂu- ence to resistance comes from recycled coarse aggregates, the greatest reduction being caused by red ceramic aggregates. This can be explained by the shape of the ceramic aggregate, which has a more angular appearance and does not allow adequate pack- ing of the grains. This results in concretes with a greater amount of voids [20]. Another factor to be considered is that the resistance of the ceramic aggregate is lower than that of other recycled aggre- gates. In this research the greater CRA inﬂuence on compressive strength may be linked to the composition, since it presents a greater amount of ceramics and mortar than concrete and rocks. Compressive strength is increased upon reduction of the w/c ra- tio and m, and is more signiﬁcant for w/c ( Fig. 8b). The analysis of m (a + p) as a function of the w/c ratio shows that with the increase of the recycled aggregate level there is an increased water demand in the concretes, which elevates the w/c ratio of the mixture and consequently reduces resistance. According to the analysis of the response surfaces a 50% FRA and 50% CRA content for concretes with w/c ratios up to 0.60 showed satisfactory results for compressive strength. However concretes with 100% FRA without the use of CRA also performed satisfactorily. The response surfaces regarding tensile strength are shown in Fig. 9 . The same tendency as for compressive strength is shown on surface 9a, i.e., the increased recycled aggregate content tends to reduce the resistance of concretes, the coarse aggregate having a greater negative inﬂuence on this property. The reduction of ten- sile strength compared to the reference concrete is of 18% for 100

found for compressive strength and for tensile strength, i.e., that the increased content of recycled aggregate (ﬁne and coarse) tends to reduce the resistance of concretes and also the elastic modulus. This reduction is practically the same for both aggregates, about 30%. According to Mehta and Monteiro[21], dense aggregates have a high deformation module and therefore a higher quantity of dense aggre- gates in a concrete mixture will correspond to a greater deformation module of the concrete. Since the recycled aggregates, both coarse and ﬁne, are more porous and less dense than natural aggregates, they can be said to diminish the concrete deformation module, as conﬁrmed in the experimental results obtained. Also, when the recy- cled aggregate content increases, the compressive strength and the elastic modulus diminish [22,23]. The authors also mention that retraction due to drying increases (even though it was not checked in this study, various authors established that this property is about 30% higher for concretes produced with recycled aggregate). Fig. 4. Effect of the replacement of the recycled aggregates on the concrete properties. Fig. 5. Observed values /C2 waste generated. Fig. 6. Evolution of carbonation for the reference concrete ( m = 4.38): (a) 7 days; (b) 14 days; (c) 28 days; (d) 56 days. P.S. Lovato et al. / Construction and Building Materials 26 (2012) 437–447 443

Therefore the authors recommend that, before the dosage of the concrete, the effects of the aggregate content and quality should be considered in the ﬁnal quality of the concrete. Analyzing the response surfaces it is observed that a content of 50% FRA and 50% CRA presents satisfactory results as to the elastic modulus as well as concretes containing only FRA at higher levels, lower than 90%. This behavior is also similar to that veriﬁed for compressive strength. Fig. 10b shows that the elastic modulus rises when the w/c ratio and m are lowered, and is more signiﬁcantly inﬂuenced by the w/c variation, a behavior also observed for compressive strength. Likewise, it is seen that the elastic modulus shows a satisfactory performance when the w/c ratios are between 0.5 and 0.6, inde- pendent of the value of m. 3.2.2. Durability properties Fig. 11 presents the response surfaces concerning the water absorption of the concretes. Surface (a) shows that as the recycled aggregate content increases, the water absorption of the concretes also increases, although only by a small percentage (about 1%). Sur- face (b) shows that water absorption increases with the elevation of the w/c ratio. However, the increase of m has low relationship Fig. 7. Evolution of carbonation for the mixture with (100% FRA; 100% CRA; m = 4.38): (a) 7 days; (b) 14 days; (c) 28 days; (d) 56 days. Fig. 8. Response surface to axial compressive strength as a function of the aggregates (a) and the w/c /C2 m (b) ratio. Fig. 9. Response surface for tensile strength as a function of the aggregates (a) and the w/c /C2 m (b) ratio. 444 P.S. Lovato et al. / Construction and Building Materials 26 (2012) 437–447

to this property. Hence, a satisfactory performance is shown for water absorption when employing 50% of CRA and FRA, an inter- mediate m value and a w/c ratio of up to 0.65. As shown in Fig. 12 a, the increased content of recycled aggre- gates, both ﬁne and coarse, did not have much inﬂuence on the car- bonation depth of concrete. However, concretes produced with recycled aggregates tend to present slightly higher rates of carbon- ation depth (about 5 mm) than the reference. Concretes with 100% CRA and FRA present carbonation depth rates that are about 43% higher than those produced with 100% natural aggregates [22] also found similar carbonation depth rates in concretes produced with recycled and natural aggregates. On the other hand [12,24] found carbonation depths about 1.3–3.5 times higher for concrete pro- duced with recycled aggregate. However, the higher the m and the w/c ratio the greater the concrete carbonation depths ( Fig. 12b). The inﬂuence of the w/c ra- tio on the carbonation depth of concrete is a consensus in litera- ture, and is related to the cementing matrix porosity and to the ease of CO 2 entry though the concrete. Contrary to the mechanical properties evaluated as to water absorption durability and carbonation properties, concrete behav- ior is more negatively affected by the use of recycled ﬁne aggre- gates than by recycled coarse aggregates. The increased water absorption of concretes as compared to the reference concrete is 44.5% for 100% of FRA and 15% for 100% of CRA. In the concretes produced by [3] carbonation depth also was more inﬂuenced by recycled ﬁne aggregate. The CO 2 diffusion through concrete occurs mainly through the interconnected capillary pores, as well as through the microﬁssures or air bubbles in the matrix. Certain por- ous aggregates also can be part of the transport system [25]. This difference in the inﬂuence of the CRAs and FRAs on the mechanical and durability properties, is linked respectively to the characteristics of the aggregates, since the resistance of the coarse aggregate is essentially important for the compressive strength. As the recycled aggregates are less resistant than the natural ones, breaks can occur in the aggregate itself; for instance, the aggregate of the reference concrete (basalt) shows a resistance of about 290 MPa [26], while the average resistance estimated for the recy- cled aggregates employed is approximately 10 MPa. As to durability, the transition zone and the porosity of hydrated cement paste and of the aggregates predominate, since the access of the aggressive agents occurs through them. Leite [11] observed that in concrete with recycled aggregates the matrix and transition zone are practically undistinguishable. Besides, the hydration products cover the surfaces of the recycled aggregates, densifying the transition zone. The author proved that hydration products are formed on the rough and porous surface of the aggre- gate due to the absorption of the cement by the recycled material. However this pore closing effect and improvement of the transition zone is usually annulled by the greater demand for water in con- cretes with recycled aggregates. 3.3. Cement consumption and cost/m 3 The ideal recycled aggregate content in concrete, besides the most appropriate contents veriﬁed on the response surfaces for each property, will also take into account the cost/m 3 relation of concrete. In Fig. 13 the consumption values of cement and cost/ m3 for some combin

resistance as the reference concrete. Therefore the savings achieved by using recycled aggregates are practically annulled by the cost of cement, and in view of this all the concretes present a similar cost/m 3. However, this practice yields environmental ben- eﬁts and should be considered from this perspective. The results of this study also show that when natural aggregate contents are replaced by C&D aggregates of up to 50% the concretes produced are technically feasible, and that it is possible to produce concretes with a resistance of about 25 MPa, with a good mechanical and durability performance. Therefore, from a technical, economic and environmental point of view it is possible to produce concretes from construction and demolition aggregates. It should be stressed that due to the increased water consumption of concretes with C&D aggregates and the low mechanical resistance of recycled coarse aggregates special attention should be dedicated to the durability properties and to the elastic modulus of these concretes. 4. Conclusions Regarding the mechanical and durability properties of concrete produced with recycled aggregate from constructions and demoli- tions, based on the results of this research it is possible to conclude that: /C15 The increased content of recycled aggregates, both coarse and ﬁne, causes a loss of the mechanical properties of the material. Compressive strength, tensile strength and the elastic modulus are lower in concretes with recycled aggregates than in the ref- erence concrete, the recycled coarse aggregate (RCA) having a greater negative inﬂuence than the recycled ﬁne aggregate (RFA). Special attention must be given to the elastic modulus which presents a drop of 44.8%, in relation to the reference concrete. /C15 The durability properties are also affected by the increment of recycled aggregates in concrete. Carbonation depth and water retention are higher in concretes with recycled aggregates, especially for ﬁne aggregates, and this is not desirable. The cause is usually the fact that the water demand, when produc- ing concrete with recycled aggregates is higher for the same workability, increasing the water/cement ratio, thereby, also increasing the porosity of the cementing matrix, and conse- quently reducing the durability of these concretes. The use of water reducing additives may minimize this effect, since they may provide workability to the mixture without increasing the water/cement ratio. /C15 From the technical point of view concretes with 50% substitu- tion of natural aggregates by recycled aggregates, may achieve compressive strength up to 25 MPa, with carbonation depths similar to that of the reference concrete, as long as the water/ cement ratio is not over 0.60. From the environmental point of view these concretes allow a 50% reduction in the consump- tion of ﬁne and coarse natural aggregates, which contributes positively for the sustainability of this area. From the economic point of view, for the same axial compressive strength level, higher than 20 MPa, despite the greater cement consumption, the costs are similar to those of the reference concrete. For lower strengths levels, concretes produced with construction and demolition residue reduce the costs of concrete production in about 20%. /C15 Thus, through analyses using surface response methodology (SRM), it was determined that concretes produced with up to 50% substitution by ﬁne and coarse natural aggregates by C&D aggregates are

Modeling of mechanical properties and durability of recycled aggregate concretes Patrícia S. Lovato a, Edna Possan b,⇑, Denise Carpena Coitinho Dal Molin a,c, Ângela B. Masuero a, José Luis D. Ribeiro d a Counseling Center for Innovation of Buildings of Federal University of Rio Grande do Sul (NORIE/UFRGS), Brazil b Federal Technological University of Paraná (UTFPR), Advanced Study Center in Security of Hydroelectric Dams of the Technological Plant of Itaipu (C EASB/PTI), Brazil c Engineering School of UFRGS, Brazil d Federal University of Rio Grande do Sul (UFRGS), Brazil article info Article history: Received 17 February 2011 Received in revised form 10 June 2011 Accepted 18 June 2011 Available online 20 July 2011 Keywords: Recycled aggregate Durability Mechanical properties C&D waste Response surface methodology abstract Growing concern with the preservation of non-renewable natural resources has led to the development of studies on the use of recycled aggregates in concrete production. In view of the diversity and variability of these aggregates there is a lack of consensus on the performance of these concretes. This study uses response surface methodology (RSM), to jointly evaluate the mechanical and durability properties (water absorption and carbonation depth) of concretes produced with different levels of ﬁne and coarse aggre- gates, the C&D waste, for different w/c ratios. Concrete production costs were also evaluated. The results show that the increasing of the recycled aggregate, both ﬁne and coarse, increases the w/c ratio, water absorption and carbonation depth of concrete. Coarse recycled aggregate has a greater inﬂuence on the mechanical properties, while ﬁne recycled aggregate affects the concrete durability properties. Fifty percentage of substitution by ﬁne and coarse C&D aggregates is feasible from a technical, economic and environmental point of view. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction 1.1. Concretes with wastes Factors such as maintenance programming, life cycle cost, sus- tainable use of natural resources and energy have elicited in- creased attention when drawing up projects and managing enterprises in the Construction Industry (CI). On the other hand, this industry, which is one of the main consumers of natural re- sources (energy and materials), and is also responsible for 30% of the CO 2 emissions into the environment and about 40% of man- produced waste [1], shows a high potential for residue utilization. The cement industry, in particular, already uses waste from the production of silicium (silica fume), steel (blast furnace slag) and paper (meta-kaolin), thermoelectric power plants (ﬂy ash) and agribusiness (rice husk ash). Another residue that can potentially be used for concrete production, although it is not yet widespread, is construction and demolition residue (C&D), since recycling it is an alternative to diminish the impact of energy and raw material consumption on the environment. It is estimated that the European Union generates yearly between 200 and 300 million tons of C&D waste, which is equiva- lent to 0.5–1 ton/inhab/year [2]. Data presented by Shayan and Xu [3] show that in Australia more than 3 million tons of C&D are pro- duced per year, most of which are concrete residue. Approximately 50% of these are recycled to produce coarse aggregates. In Hong Kong the CI produces around 37,000 tons of C&D per day, repre- senting 38% of the waste generated [4,5]. Brazil genera
